data.title: MakeMyTrip Non-GAAP EPS of $0.52 beats by $0.10, revenue of $295.69M misses by $10.4M
data.description:
• MakeMyTrip press release (MMYT): Q3 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.52 beats by $0.10.
  • Revenue of $295.69M (+10.6% Y/Y) misses by $10.4M.
  • Gross Bookings increased by 11.8% Y/Y in 3Q26 to $2,784.5 million.
  • Adjusted Margin – Air Ticketing increased by 20.4% Y/Y in 3Q26 to $107.9 million.
  • Adjusted Margin– Hotels and Packages increased by 14.6% YoY in 3Q26 to $133.2 million.
  • Adjusted Margin– Bus Ticketing increased by 26.1% Y/Y in 3Q26 to $42.4 million.
  • Adjusted Margin – Others increased by 45.5% Y/Y in 3Q26 to $27.5 million.

data.title: Elon Musk's most boring company is making a lot of noise in Nevada
data.description:
Elon Musk's lowest-profile company is still making a lot of noise. The Boring Company is working with the Economic Development Authority of Western Nevada on a conceptual plan for a tunnel running roughly nine miles under Interstate 80 from Reno to the Tesla (TSLA) Gigafactory at the Tahoe-Reno Industrial Center, funded by a $50,000 feasibility and conceptual design contract.

The study on the potential for a Boring tunnel is one of several options being considered by transportation officials to ease worsening congestion and accidents on I-80 near Reno, as employment at the Tahoe Reno Industrial Center has surged to about 22,000 workers, including thousands at Tesla (TSLA) and Panasonic.

The Boring Company is also active in Las Vegas, with the Vegas Loop experiencing significant expansion in early 2026 as the transportation system evolves from its original convention center focus into a comprehensive citywide transit network. The network currently has eight operational stations in the Strip corridor, including five at the Las Vegas Convention Center, plus stations at Resorts World (opened in 2022), Westgate (opened in January 2025), and Encore (opened in April 2025). The most anticipated development is the expanding airport connection to Harry Reid International Airport, scheduled to continue growing to a capacity to transport up to 20,000 people per hour.

A 2022 Series C funding round valued The Boring Company at $5.7 billion.

Caterpillar (CAT), AECOM (ACM), and Jacobs Engineering Group (J) are three companies that are very loosely related to Boring's unique main business. Elon Musk's other companies include Tesla (TSLA), SpaceX (SPACE), Neuralink, and xAI (XAI).

data.title: Xerox announces $250M mixed shelf offering
data.description:
• Xerox (XRX) intends to offer, in one or more offerings, up to $250M aggregate principal amount of the securities consisting of stocks and warrants.
  • The use of the net proceeds from the sale will be mentioned later in the prospectus supplement.
  • Source

data.title: J&J posts Q4 revenue beat as Pharma, MedTech units outperform
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), the earnings bellwether for the healthcare sector, reported better-than-expected revenue for Q4 2025 on Tuesday thanks to its Pharma and MedTech divisions. However, the shares of the New Brunswick, New Jersey-based healthcare giant slipped ~2% in the premarket as its adjusted earnings for the quarter did not exceed the consensus.

“2025 was a catapult year for Johnson & Johnson, fueled by the strongest portfolio and pipeline in our history,” CEO Joaquin Duato remarked ahead of the earnings call at 8:30 a.m. ET.

The company reported $24.6B in revenue for the quarter compared to $24.16B in the consensus, while its adjusted earnings for Q4 stood at $2.46 per share, in line with analysts’ expectations.

As for segmental performance, J&J’s (JNJ) Innovative Medicine and MedTech units added $15.8B and $8.8B to the topline, exceeding the $15.45B and $8.67B projected by analysts, respectively, according to Bloomberg data.

This is a developing story. Check back for more updates.

data.title: Brand Engagement Network enters $2.05B AI licensing partnership in Africa
data.description:
• Brand Engagement Network (BNAI) announced a $2.05M AI licensing partnership with Valio Technologies for government and commercial markets across Africa.
  • Key terms of the deal include a $2.05B preferred equity contribution from a newly formed South Africa–based entity to the company, 25% common equity ownership in the newly formed entity, 35% revenue share across software, SaaS, services, and subscription offerings, and exclusive license for government and private-sector markets across Africa.

data.title: Meta's new AI team delivered first key models internally this month - report
data.description:
• Meta Platforms' (META) new artificial intelligence lab has delivered its first high-profile AI models internally this month, the company's chief technology officer told Reuters on Wednesday.
  • At a press briefing on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum's annual meeting in Davos, CTO Andrew Bosworth said the models built by its Meta Superintelligence Labs team, formed last year, showed a lot of promise.
  • "They're basically six months into the work, not quite even," Bosworth said, adding that the team's AI models were "very good". Bosworth, known as "Boz," did not specify which models were delivered internally.
  • Speaking generally about the development cycle, Bosworth said: "There's a tremendous amount of work to do post-training" for AI, "to actually deliver the model in a way that's usable internally and by consumers."

data.title: SCHMID Group announces $30M convertible notes financing
data.description:
SCHMID Group (SHMD) said it has signed an investment agreement with an institutional investor to sell $30M of senior convertible notes, which can be converted into ordinary shares, along with warrants to purchase ordinary shares.

The notes will bear interest at 7% per annum and will mature on January 21, 2028, unless previously converted into shares of the company.

They will be issued pursuant to an indenture at 98% of principal amount and are funded in two tranches: (i) $15M will be funded two business days after the execution of the investment agreement on January 21, 2026, and (ii) $15M will be funded following the effectiveness of a registration statement covering the resale of the underlying shares in relation to the notes.

The notes are convertible, at the option of the investor, into shares of the company at prices determined by reference to fixed premium conversion prices, including at 95% of the applicable volume-weighted average price of the shares, subject to a minimum conversion price and certain daily conversion limits as further specified in the investment agreement.

Net proceeds will be used for general corporate purposes, including working capital, capital expenditures, and potential acquisitions or investments.

SHMD +4.35% premarket to $9.59

Source: Press Release

data.title: Deutsche Bank disavows research note amid White House pressure
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent revealed Wednesday that Deutsche Bank’s chief executive personally called to distance the German lender from a controversial analyst report suggesting European investors might sell off American assets.

Speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Bessent disclosed that Deutsche Bank CEO Christian Sewing reached out to dismiss findings from one of the bank’s own researchers, Bloomberg reported.

“This notion that Europeans would be selling US assets came from a single analyst at Deutsche Bank,” Bessent said. “The CEO of Deutsche Bank called to say that Deutsche Bank does not stand by that analyst report.”

The note in question, authored by Global Head of FX Research George Saravelos, argued that Europe may become less willing to hold U.S. assets amid President Donald Trump’s threats over Greenland. Saravelos suggested such dollar rebalancing would likely limit negative impacts on the euro.

European countries own $8T in U.S. bonds (TBT) (TLT) (SHY) (IEF) (IEI) (BIL) and equities (SPY) (QQQ) (DIA), nearly twice as much as the rest of the world combined.

Deutsche Bank’s unusual move to distance itself from its own research underscores the heightened sensitivity around the topic, particularly for a German lender with substantial U.S. operations.

A Deutsche Bank spokesperson declined to confirm the communication between Sewing and government officials to Bloomberg, but emphasized that “Deutsche Bank Research is independent in their work, therefore views expressed in individual research notes do not necessarily represent the view of the bank’s management.”

"Very depressing a) that the admin gets so worked up and b) that the bank wouldn't stand by its own analyst," Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at IG, posted.

Saravelos’ report noted that some European institutions have already begun reducing dollar exposure. Danish pension fund AkademikerPension, which manages around $25 billion in savings, announced Tuesday it plans to exit U.S. Treasuries by month’s end, citing credit risks created by Trump administration policies.

"While media headlines have focused on 'sell US” trades,' this is misleading," UBS' Paul Donovan wrote. "It is not selling the stock of debt that creates a fiscal crisis, but the funding inflow." 

"The UK’s Truss debacle and the Greek financial crisis were essentially funding problems. Reduced inflows rather than mass selling are the real risk. This distinction is relevant for the US (dependent on foreign funding flows) and Japan (domestically financed)."
